name: Platform-neutral procurement language for AltID integration
consequences: Direct effect: Shifts procurement toward platform-neutral safeguards and explicit fallback requirements, creating formal guardrails that reduce single-vendor risk. Downstream, procurement cycles may lengthen and incumbent operators resist changes, while standardization accelerates interoperability and EU alignment but introduces new negotiation complexity.
options: Codify platform-neutral fallback requirements into Digitaliseringsstyrelsen procurement templates, including interoperability criteria, fallback authentication paths, and sunset timelines aligned with AltID roadmap.
Create an independent continuity and supplier-concentration oversight board empowered to assess vendor risk, mandate mitigation plans, and publish annual resilience reports.
Mandate EU-aligned NSIS and eIDAS-based interoperability standards across all future procurements, with explicit fallback authentication tests during vendor evaluations.
review: Core tension: procurement-imposed platform-neutrality must survive political and industry pushback while still delivering credible continuity. Weakness: the options miss an explicit mechanism to enforce ongoing compliance and to fund transition costs across multiple procurement cycles.

name: Dependency-surface discovery and resilience risk mapping
lever_classification: methodology — how to map the dependency surface and assess risks
consequences: Direct effect: Establishes a centralized, living view of where MitID depends on non-native components, enabling targeted mitigations and prioritization of fallback paths. Downstream, teams must maintain data governance and update cycles; risk of analysis paralysis if governance becomes slow; may delay procurement decisions due to ongoing risk reclassification.
options: Develop a live dependency surface registry that catalogs MitID platform integrity signals, app-distribution channels, and telemetry dependencies with owned risk scores.
Apply scenario-based threat modeling to identify failure modes and quantify resilience gaps using qualitative narratives and accessible matrices.
Pilot modular architecture studies to evaluate decoupled components enabling rapid fallback anchor switching without producing production-grade components.
review: Core tension: platform-neutral risk mapping must balance completeness with privacy and governance constraints. Weakness: the options do not specify ownership of the registry or sustainable funding for ongoing maintenance.

name: Phase-two demonstrator sequencing and scope discipline
lever_classification: execution — how to sequence demonstrators and define scope
consequences: Direct effect: Demonstrates the feasibility of non-platform-dependent authentication through non-production demonstrators, building credibility for a fallback path. Downstream, findings will inform procurement language and standards discussions, but the non-production scope may limit regulatory acceptance and require careful risk framing.
options: Structure the phase-two demonstrators with clear milestones for mobile-oriented WebAuthn flows on privacy-respecting devices, plus external validation by independent experts.
Implement a browser-based or hardware-token fallback pathway that operates without relying on platform integrity signals, using open standards to prove feasibility.
Establish an independent evaluation framework to test fallback paths under simulated platform outages and policy shifts, with documented pass/fail criteria.
review: Core tension: proving feasibility without production-scale integration may overstate readiness. Weakness: the options lack a concrete plan for eventual production integration with MitID’s certification framework.

name: Stakeholder engagement and policy translation for resilience
lever_classification: dissemination — how to engage stakeholders and translate findings
consequences: Direct effect: expands legitimacy and political buy-in for resilience initiatives; downstream, broad engagement may trigger additional scrutiny on privacy and democratic accountability, potentially slowing decisions but increasing long-term acceptance.
options: Publish accessible policy memos and briefs that translate technical findings into procurement language for civil servants and lawmakers.
Coordinate formal policy dossiers and public briefings to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and parliamentary digital policy committees.
Organize multi-stakeholder workshops and online forums to align expectations across civil society, privacy advocates, and industry.
review: Core tension: communicating technical risk without oversimplification; Weakness: the options may not ensure consistent messaging across agencies, risking mixed interpretations.

name: Operations governance and phased funding gating
lever_classification: operations — how to run the program day-to-day and gating
consequences: Direct effect: ensures disciplined progress through independent reviews and defined gating points, reducing scope creep. Downstream, administrative overhead increases and decision cycles may slow; risk of constraining rapid pivots if new evidence emerges.
options: Establish quarterly risk reviews with independent security auditors to validate deliverables and maintain governance discipline.
Set up a Copenhagen-based secure workspace with controlled access for security-sensitive meetings, demonstrations, and stakeholder briefings.
Institute a strict phased funding gating mechanism requiring published feasibility, formal engagements, and a financing plan before advancing phases.
review: Core tension: gating can slow critical pivots; Weakness: options do not articulate incentives for timely procurement alignment or escalation procedures if gates stall.

name: EU wallet interoperability alignment and standards pressure
lever_classification: dissemination — influence EU standards and interoperability language
consequences: Direct effect: positions Denmark within EU interoperability trajectories, increasing credibility with EU bodies and suppliers. Downstream, ongoing EU engagement consumes time and resources, and regulatory timelines may lag national urgency, potentially delaying domestic momentum.
options: Engage with EU bodies to push for platform-neutrality language in European Digital Identity Wallet materials and interoperability guidelines.
Develop a cross-border interoperability appendix to AltID-related design documents to facilitate EU procurement and standards alignment.
Advocate for certified fallback mechanisms within EU frameworks, ensuring member states can implement platform-neutral access without vendor lock-in.
review: Core tension: EU consensus may slow national progress; Weakness: options rely on EU processes that are not binding and could be slower than domestic timelines.

name: Procurement language realignment for AltID platform-neutral access
lever_classification: governance — procurement terms defining platform-neutral access and fallback requirements
consequences: Direct effect: procurement terms will require consideration of platform-neutral access and certified fallback paths in bids. Downstream: suppliers must adapt architectures and documentation, potentially extending procurement cycles and increasing integration costs; risk: pushback from incumbents could delay milestone delivery if terms are not harmonized with existing contracts.
options: Redraft AltID-related procurement clauses to require platform-neutral access and certified fallback paths as primary evaluation criteria in all upcoming bids.
Convene an independent procurement oversight board to audit contracts for lock-in risk and enforce contingency terms with non-Apple/Google providers.
Pilot a short-cycle procurement track that tests platform-neutral quote evaluation using a standardized fallback authentication prototype within NSIS-compliant testbeds.
review: Core tension: procurement reform often moves slowly and unevenly across ministries, risking a gap between policy intent and contract practice. Weakness: the options miss explicit enforcement mechanisms or a plan to monitor compliance and adapt terms as markets evolve.

name: Open-standards demonstrator program for fallback authentication
lever_classification: execution — how to sequence the demonstrator development phases
consequences: Direct effect: demonstrators provide credible evidence that a standards-based fallback flow can authenticate users without Apple/Google trust anchors. Downstream: findings inform procurement language and policy decisions, but may reveal integration gaps requiring additional funding and timeline extensions; risk: results may be inconclusive if testbeds fail to capture real-world usage.
options: Commission a cross-domain demonstrator that implements FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS devices and a privacy-respecting browser path to validate interoperability.
Create a parallel browser-based or hardware-token fallback prototype and run a controlled pilot to compare user experience and security properties against a mobile-centric baseline.
Engage independent security labs to perform threat modeling and penetration testing across the demonstrators, reporting findings publicly to accelerate governance decisions.
review: Core tension: demonstration results may not directly translate to procurement language and deployment readiness; Weakness: options do not specify how success is measured or what constitutes credible evidence, risking biased conclusions.

name: Policy coalition and advocacy program for continuity-of-service protection
lever_classification: dissemination — how to shape public and EU policy discourse
consequences: Direct effect: builds legitimacy for platform-neutral governance; Downstream: attention may trigger political friction and potential misalignment with public admin cycles; risk: without binding commitments, advocacy may not convert into procurement changes.
options: Formalize a coalition charter and publish a policy brief urging Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to embed resilience criteria in AltID procurement.
Schedule a series of parliamentary briefings and civil-society roundtables to scrutinize dependency on platform ecosystems and elevate fallback options.
Co-develop a public-interest scoring framework that quantifies supplier concentration risks and publish quarterly updates tied to procurement milestones.
review: Core tension: advocacy can be politicized, potentially delaying actions; Weakness: options do not specify how to maintain coalition coherence or handle conflicting stakeholder demands.

name: Supplier diversification and contingency arrangements for identity verification
lever_classification: operations — how to structure vendor relationships and continuity controls
consequences: Direct effect: reduces dependence on a single vendor and creates defined contingency pathways; Downstream: increases governance overhead and potential integration fragmentation; trade-off: higher ongoing maintenance and cross-vendor compatibility costs.
options: Negotiate multi-vendor framework agreements with alternative platform providers to ensure turnkey fallback capabilities and service credits in case of disruption.
Institute binding contingency commitments within contracts, requiring clear switch-over timelines, data portability, and verified post-switch rollback procedures.
Develop a cold-start standby identity verifier with government-approved credentials that can be activated within days if platform signals fail.
review: Core tension: multi-vendor setups raise integration and support costs; Weakness: options do not specify governance for data portability, sovereignty, or cross-vendor credential revocation across jurisdictions.

name: EU NSIS alignment and interoperable wallet integration push
lever_classification: dissemination — coordinating EU-level standards and policy input
consequences: Direct effect: aligns Danish requirements with European NSIS wallet interoperability and eIDAS-based fallbacks; Downstream: procurement cycles may shift to meet EU milestones and influence national vendor choices; risk: domestic policy windows may lag behind European developments.
options: Submit formal comments to the EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups and align Danish procurement language with NSIS-based portability and resilience criteria.
Pilot cross-border interoperability tests with partner states to validate platform-neutral flows and publish comparative results to inform national policy.
Draft a Danish NSIS-compliant procurement annex that translates platform-neutrality into measurable, auditable requirements for future contracts.
review: Core tension: EU alignment can be slow and uncertain; Weakness: options assume EU momentum will drive domestic changes; risk that national procurement cycles outrun EU updates.

name: Credential-stack certification pathway and government-backed verification
lever_classification: product — what fallback credential approach to prototype
consequences: Direct effect: establishes a government-backed certification path for fallback credential stacks that can be used in procurement; Downstream: requires establishing certification bodies, test regimes, and recertification; risk: adds governance overhead and potential delays to procurement if certification cycles lag.
options: Co-develop a government-backed certification program for platform-neutral fallback authentication stacks, including test suites and interoperability criteria.
Engage accredited labs to perform third-party interoperability testing across devices, browsers, and OS variants and publish test results.
Create an incremental certification roadmap that aligns with procurement milestones and budgets, allowing phased inclusion of fallback capabilities.
review: Core tension: certification adds governance and time-to-market overhead; Weakness: options do not specify funding sources or independence of cert bodies, or how to handle recertification.

name: Fallback-authentication governance and sponsor network construction
lever_classification: governance — who oversees the fallback-authentication pilots and funding oversight
consequences: Pulling this lever creates a formal governance and funding channel for fallback-authentication pilots, enabling coordinated experiments and shared risk with clear milestones. The downstream implications include longer decision cycles due to multi-stakeholder alignment and potential budget contention if milestones slip, but it builds legitimacy for platform-neutral options.
options: Formalize a government–civil-society–industry consortium to oversee fallback authentication pilots, with clear milestones, reporting requirements, and budget oversight.
Launch a regulatory sandbox for platform-neutral authentication pilots, allowing phased testing in controlled public-sector environments while maintaining MitID compatibility constraints.
Establish a performance-based procurement framework that ties continuity metrics to contractor incentives for platform-neutral components and fallback pathways.
review: Core tension centers on balancing speed to pilot with durable governance. A weakness the options miss is how to sustain momentum if coalition actors pull back or diverge on milestone timing; none of the options explicitly specify performance metrics for fallback functionality or sunset criteria.

name: Open-reference interoperability blueprint for platform-neutral wallet integration
lever_classification: product — define an open-reference interoperability blueprint and integration points for platform-neutral wallet
consequences: Adopting an open-reference interoperability blueprint creates a shared baseline for platform-neutral wallet interfaces and testing, accelerating credible demonstrations and reducing lock-in. Downstream, incumbents may resist open interfaces, and governance of the blueprint could delay formal certification workflows if ownership is unclear.
options: Publish a government-backed open-reference interoperability blueprint that defines a platform-neutral wallet interface compatible with NSIS and eIDAS using FIDO2/WebAuthn.
Commission cross-industry hackathons to produce compatible, audit-ready code samples and test harnesses that demonstrate fallback pathways across devices.
Launch a staged rollout of the reference implementation as a non-production pilot with strict privacy safeguards and separate data environments.
review: Core tension lies between openness for interoperability and control over certification risk; a potential weakness is that an open blueprint may advance before a formal certification path is established, leaving ambiguity around liability and interoperability guarantees.

name: Procurement clauses for platform-neutral resilience and continuity contingencies
lever_classification: execution — craft resilience-focused procurement clauses within AltID contracts
consequences: This lever injects resilience deliverables into AltID procurement terms, creating accountability for fallback readiness. Downstream, it may increase procurement complexity and extend timelines, as thorough resilience criteria require additional review and alignment across agencies.
options: Incorporate resilience-focused requirements into AltID procurement documents, specifying fallback authentication readiness as a contractual deliverable.
Mandate independent security review milestones for any platform-neutral component prior to procurement approval, by external auditors with defined criteria and publish the outcomes.
Adopt a two-track procurement approach: feature-lean demonstrators and separate backbone for continuity provisions to ensure parallel progress without compromising core deliverables.
review: Core tension: resilience may raise cost and extend timelines; a weakness is that external reviews could delay procurement if schedules conflict with internal policy cycles, potentially slowing progress toward fallback readiness.

name: EU-aligned standards engagement and formal position papers
lever_classification: dissemination — coordinate EU standards engagement and public-positioning
consequences: Pursuing EU-aligned standards and formal positions grants legitimacy and cross-border momentum for platform-neutrality, improving interoperability prospects. Downstream, EU processes can be slow and broad, potentially diluting national nuance and delaying domestic procurement actions.
options: Submit formal responses to European Digital Identity Wallet consultations advocating certified fallback paths and interoperability signals beyond platform trust anchors.
Attend and present at ENISA and standards bodies to push platform-neutral interface specifications with measurable portability.
Coordinate with other member states to publish a joint whitepaper on continuity risk and platform-neutral identity infrastructure.
review: Core tension: EU alignment can slow progress due to bureaucratic timelines; a weakness is that generic European positions may fail to secure fast domestic traction unless complemented by national execution plans.

name: Independent risk mapping of platform dependency and supplier concentration
lever_classification: governance — establish independent risk mapping and oversight of supplier concentration
consequences: This lever makes risk exposure visible to policymakers and buyers, informing decision-making and procurement priorities. Downstream, risk maps can generate risk-averse behavior and political friction if not paired with credible mitigation funding, and they require ongoing updates as platform policies evolve.
options: Publish an independent risk map detailing MitID dependency on platform signals, with scenarios and continuity-impact analyses.
Develop a red-teaming exercise to stress-test fallback paths under platform policy shifts and geopolitical disruptions.
Establish a vulnerability disclosure program focused on the fallback pathway and open-standards interfaces, with clear reporting timelines and response commitments.
review: Core tension: risk mapping may prompt caution and pushback; a weakness is that without funded mitigations, risk insights may sit unused, failing to translate into practical continuity improvements.

name: Continuity-oriented procurement gating tied to milestone-based reviews
lever_classification: execution — implement milestone-based funding gates tied to fallback capabilities
consequences: Linking funding to milestone-based reviews enforces discipline toward platform-neutral capabilities, reducing continuity risk. However, gates can slow momentum if external reviews lag or if scope remains too restrictive, potentially pushing work into prolonged cycles.
options: Link phase gates to external feasibility validations, ensuring continued funding only after achieving concrete fallback-capability milestones.
Integrate independent security certification milestones for platform-neutral components before each funding tranche, reducing regulatory churn.
Institute a sunset clause that early-prunes platform-reliant dependencies if fallback not credible by phase two review.
review: Core tension: governance discipline vs agile progress; a weakness is that reliance on external reviews may introduce delays beyond project control, risking phase slippage without built-in contingency.
